user_request: Draft a commercial suit seeking damages for illegal termination of dealership agreement. Plaintiff invested substantial capital and developed territory market. Termination was arbitrary and contrary to agreement terms. Claim compensation for loss of profit, goodwill and unsold stock. Draft with proper breach of contract pleading.

facts.summary: Plaintiff seeks damages for illegal termination of a commercial dealership agreement. The plaintiff invested capital and developed the market, but claims termination was arbitrary and breached contract terms. Compensation is sought for loss of profit, goodwill, and unsold stock.

slots.value: Compensation for loss of profit, goodwill, and unsold stock

assumptions: Jurisdiction is Civil Court based on monetary valuation of damages
Termination occurred without valid cause as per agreement terms

legal_research.summary:
[Statute of limitations in contract claims : an overview - iPleaders] Under normal circumstances, the Indian Limitation Act of 1963, under Section 14 of the Limitation Act, provides that a claim for breach of contract must be made within three years from the date of such breach .
[The Period of Limitation For A Suit For Compensation For The Breach of Any Contract | PDF | Breach Of Contract | Damages] It is three years from the date the contract was broken, or from the date of the most recent breach if there were successive breaches, or from the date the continuing breach ceased .
[Applicabilty of Limitation Act to Indian Contract Act] In other words, if there is no specific provision in the Limitation Act or any other law that prescribes a limitation period for a particular type of suit, then the plaintiff can file such a suit within three years from the date when the right ...
[1 Limitation Periods: Overview (India)] Limitation Act prescribes separate periods for the preference of suit in tort, and for breach of · obligations under contract.
[Dhapai vs Dalla And Ors. on 2 April, 1969] Three years When the contract is broken, or (where there are successive breaches) when the breach in respect of which the suit is instituted occurs or (where the breach is continuing) when it ceases . 120. Suit for which no period of limitation is provided elsewhere in this Schedule.
[The Plaint: Foundational Document in Civil Litigation under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 - Bhatt & Joshi Associates] Order IV, Rule 1 of the CPC mandates that “ Every suit shall be instituted by presenting a plaint in duplicate to the Court or such officer as it appoints in this behalf ” and that “Every plaint shall comply with the rules contained in Orders ...
[Strict Compliance with Section 80 of the Civil Procedure Code for Suits Against the Government: Amar Nath Dogra v. Union of India: Supreme Court Of India | CaseMine] During the tenure of his license, Dogra alleged breaches of contractual stipulations by the Excise Authorities, leading him to serve a notice under Section 80 of the Civil Procedure Code (CPC) and subsequently file a suit claiming damages.
[Revisiting The Principles Of Damages Under The Contract Regime In India - Contracts and Commercial Law - India] The Supreme Court in M.N. Gangappa v. Atmakur Nagabhushanam Shetty & Co. and Anr., (AIR 1972 SC 696) has observed that in absence of a strict procedure for calculation of damages arising out of breach of contract, damages would be calculated based on the facts and circumstances of each case.
[Documents Filed with Plaint: Comprehensive Legal Framework and Judicial Interpretation - Bhatt & Joshi Associates] The judgment highlighted that allowing parties to introduce documents at later stages without proper justification would undermine the procedural integrity of civil suits and potentially prejudice the opposing party’s ability to prepare an adequate defense. The decision also clarified that the requirement to file documents with the plaint is not merely directory but mandatory, meaning that non-compliance carries substantive consequences.

[How to File a Civil Suit in India | Expert Tips |Kaleeyantey] A cause of action is required before filing a civil lawsuit, and it must have violated a civil right, and there shall be damage recovery or compensation in the complaint . The plaintiff will make all statements, which is the first step in the ...
[The basic court procedure for filing a civil suit in India | SoOLEGAL] File requisite amount of procedure - fee in the court. File 2 copies of plaint for each defendant in the court . Of, the 2 copies for each defendant, one shall be sent by Register/post/courier, and one by Ordinary post.
[The Plaint: Foundational Document in Civil Litigation under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 - Bhatt & Joshi Associates] The plaint thus functions as both a procedural requirement and a substantive pleading that shapes the contours of litigation from inception to resolution. Section 26 of the CPC establishes the fundamental principle governing the institution of civil suits, providing that “Every suit shall be instituted by the presentation of a plaint or in such other manner as may be prescribed.” This provision underscores the mandatory nature of the plaint in civil litigation, making it an indispensable prerequisite for the valid institution of any civil suit in Indian courts.
[Compliance Of Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act When Both Parties Refuse to Participate in the Pre-Institution Mediation - Lexology] “12A(1) – A suit which does ... that any suit in respect of a commercial dispute filed without attempting mediation would be barred by Section 12A and, therefore, liable to be rejected at the threshold itself ...
[1 THE COMMERCIAL COURTS ACT, 2015 ________ ARRANGEMENT OF SECTIONS ________] finding that it has jurisdiction to hear a commercial dispute under this Act . ... interim relief under this Act, shall not be instituted unless the plaintiff exhausts the remedy of pre- institution mediation in accordance with such manner and procedure as may be prescribed by rules made
[Exemption under Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act - Legal Position vis-à-vis IP suits - Lexology] Section 12A of the Act is reproduced hereunder: “(1) A suit, which does not contemplate any urgent interim relief under this Act, shall not be instituted unless the plaintiff exhausts the remedy of pre-institution mediation in accordance with ...
[Section 12A of Commercial Courts Act : Resolving Territorial Ambiguity in Pre-litigation Mediation – The HNLU CCLS Blog] Indian courts have reaffirmed the mandatory nature of pre-institution mediation. In Patil Automation (P) Ltd v. Rakheja Engineers, the Supreme Court categorically established the procedural mandatory character of section 12A. Likewise, in Ganga Taro Vazirani v. Deepak Raheja, the Bombay High Court emphasized the necessity for efficient dispute resolution and judicial backlog reduction, underlining the importance of pre-litigation mediation.
[Pre-litigation mediation under Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015 is mandatory to file a counterclaim in a commercial suit - Lexology] Saroj Tandon has held that pre-litigation mediation under Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015 (“Commercial Courts Act”) is a mandatory requirement, even in respect of counterclaims in a commercial dispute .

IN THE COURT OF THE {{COURT_NAME}} AT {{COURT_PLACE}}

O.S. No. __________ / 20____

{{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, Aged about {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years,
Occupation: {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}},

                                                        ... PLAINTIFF

                    VERSUS

{{DEFENDANT_NAME}},
Aged about {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years,
Occupation: {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at: {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}.

                                                        ... DEFENDANT

SUIT FOR DAMAGES FOR BREACH OF COMMERCIAL DEALERSHIP AGREEMENT AND FOR COMPENSATION OF LOSS OF PROFIT, GOODWILL AND UNSOLD STOCK UNDER SECTION 26 OF THE CPC READ WITH SECTION 12A OF THE Commercial Courts Act, 2015

PLAINTIFF: {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}, {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}. ROLE: Plaintiff.
DEFENDANT: {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}}, {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}. ROLE: Defendant.


JURISDICTION

1. The Plaintiff is a person sui juris and competent to institute the present suit.
2. This Hon'ble Court has territorial jurisdiction to try and entertain the present suit inasmuch as the Defendant carries on business at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}} within the local limits of this Court, and the cause of action arose within such territorial limits as the dealership agreement was executed and terminated within the said jurisdiction.
3. This Hon'ble Court has pecuniary jurisdiction to try the present suit, the suit being valued at Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}} (Rupees {{SUIT_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only), which is within the pecuniary limits prescribed for this Hon'ble Court.


FACTS OF THE CASE

4. The Plaintiff is a businessman engaged in commercial trading and has invested capital amounting to Rs. {{INVESTMENT_AMOUNT}} for establishing the dealership and has developed the territory market by creating local supply chain and employing staff.
5. The Defendant is the principal company and granted the Plaintiff a dealership for the territory by a written agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} executed at {{AGREEMENT_PLACE}}; the agreement provided exclusivity and termination conditions and required notice for termination.
6. The Plaintiff duly performed his obligations under the agreement, including capital investment, setting up infrastructure, recruitment of personnel, and building the market in the designated territory, and achieved the actions required under the deal as evidenced by Annexure B.
7. On or about {{TERMINATION_DATE}}, without any valid cause and contrary to the express terms of the agreement, the Defendant terminated the dealership arrangement and communicated termination in writing as per Annexure D.
8. The termination was arbitrary and not grounded in any breach by the Plaintiff; there was no material breach by the Plaintiff justifying termination under the agreement; the Defendant did not provide due process or notice under the contractual terms.
9. As a result of the termination, the Plaintiff has suffered loss including prospective profits and business opportunities in the territory, loss of goodwill, and unsold stock valued at Rs. {{UNSOLD_STOCK_VALUE}}; The total damages claimed herein are Rs. {{DAMAGES_AMOUNT}} (Rupees {{DAMAGES_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only).
10. After termination, the Plaintiff issued reminders and sought restoration or compensation; The Defendant did not respond in any satisfactory manner and maintained the termination without remedy (copies of reminders are annexed as Annexure C).
11. A legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} was served on the Defendant demanding restoration or compensation; a true copy of the notice along with postal receipts is annexed as Annexure E; The Defendant's reply dated {{REPLY_DATE}}, if any, is annexed as Annexure E.
12. The Plaintiff is left with no alternative but to approach this Hon'ble Court for relief, as the breach has harmed the Plaintiff's business and prevented the Plaintiff from recouping the investments and anticipated profits.
13. The Plaintiff asserts that the damages claimed are fair and reasonable and justified by the terms of the dealership agreement and the ordinary principles of contract law.


LEGAL BASIS

14. The termination and breach of the dealership agreement by the Defendant gives rise to a cause of action for damages under the Indian Contract Act, 1872. Section 73 provides that when a contract is broken, the party suffering by such breach is entitled to receive compensation for any loss or damage caused thereby which naturally arose in the usual course of things from such breach. The Plaintiff's loss of profits, goodwill, and unsold stock directly arise from the Defendant's breach of the agreement and are recoverable under this provision.
15. The present suit seeks damages in substitution of or in addition to performance, consistent with the contract law principle that the non-breaching party is entitled to remedial compensation for breach. (Rationale referenced to general contract law principles and supportive jurisprudence discussed in the RAG text.)
16. In addition to the above, the Plaintiff relies on the general principle of restitution and equitable considerations under applicable contract law to secure the value of the invested capital, development of the territory, and losses arising from termination.


CAUSE OF ACTION

17. ORIGIN: The cause of action first arose on {{TERMINATION_DATE}} when the Defendant terminated the dealership agreement without just cause and in breach of contract, resulting in loss of profits, goodwill, and unsold stock.
18. FURTHER ACCRUAL: The cause of action further arose on {{NOTICE_DATE}} when the Defendant failed to remedy or respond satisfactorily to the legal notice and refused to restore the dealership or compensate for the losses.
19. CONTINUING NATURE: The cause of action is a continuing one as the Defendant continues to deprive the Plaintiff of the negotiated profits, goodwill, and the value of unsold stock, thereby continuing to cause loss day by day until compensation is provided or the contract is restored.


LIMITATION

20. The suit is within the period of limitation prescribed under Article 113 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, which prescribes a period of 3 years for suits relating to breach of contract. The cause of action first arose on {{TERMINATION_DATE}}, and the suit is filed within the prescribed period.


VALUATION AND COURT FEE

21. The suit is valued at Rs. {{DAMAGES_AMOUNT}} (Rupees {{DAMAGES_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) for the purposes of jurisdiction and court fee. The requisite court fee of Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}/- has been paid on this plaint in accordance with the applicable Court Fees Act, computed on the suit valuation.


INTEREST

22. The Plaintiff claims interest as follows:
    (a) Pre-suit interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from {{TERMINATION_DATE}} to the date of filing of this suit on Rs. {{DAMAGES_AMOUNT}} (amount to be detailed in the final computation) until realization; (pre-suit amount: {{PRE_SUIT_INTEREST_AMOUNT}})
    (b) Pendente lite interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum during the pendency of this suit under Section 34 of the CPC, to be assessed by the Court as just and reasonable; 
    (c) Future interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from the date of decree till actual realization.


PRAYER

23. In the premises stated hereinabove, it is most respectfully prayed that this Hon'ble Court may be pleased to:
    (a) Pass a decree in favour of the Plaintiff and against the Defendant for Rs. {{DAMAGES_AMOUNT}} (Rupees {{DAMAGES_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) being damages for breach including loss of profits, goodwill and unsold stock;
    (b) Award pre-suit interest at Rs. {{PRE_SUIT_INTEREST_AMOUNT}} or at the rate and for the period specified above;
    (c) Award pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC at such rate as this Court may deem just and reasonable during the pendency of the suit;
    (d) Award future interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from the date of decree till realization;
    (e) Award costs of this suit including court fees and legal costs incurred in the pre-litigation process;
    (f) Grant such other and further relief as this Hon'ble Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.


LIST OF DOCUMENTS

Annexures:
A. Primary dealership agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} executed at {{AGREEMENT_PLACE}}.
B. Proof of capital investment and market development (invoices, bank statements, asset registers) annexed as Annexure B.
C. Inventory records and list of unsold stock as on termination date annexed as Annexure C.
D. Termination letter dated {{TERMINATION_DATE}} annexed as Annexure D.
E. Legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} with postal receipts and acknowledgment card annexed as Annexure E.
F. Correspondence/communications between the parties annexed as Annexure F (if any).


VERIFICATION

I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby solemnly verify and state on oath that the contents of paragraphs 1 to 23 of this plaint are true and correct to my personal knowledge based upon the records and documents available with me, that the contents relating to legal submissions are based upon legal advice received and are believed to be correct, that no part of the cause of action has arisen within the jurisdiction of any other court, and that nothing material has been concealed therefrom. Verified at {{COURT_PLACE}} on this the ____ day of __________ 20____.

                                                        {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}

                                                        PLAINTIFF

Through:
{{ADVOCATE_NAME}}
Advocate
Enrollment No. {{ADVOCATE_ENROLLMENT}}
{{ADVOCATE_ADDRESS}}
